title: Strawberry jam
subtitle: Classic British strawberry preserve
excerpt: Fresh strawberries cooked with sugar and lemon juice to a bright, glossy jam that sets firmly and tastes of the fruit. A recipe worth knowing every summer. Makes approximately four 340 g jars.
sourceNotes: Strawberry jam is the foundational British preserve, associated with July strawberry season and the Women's Institute. The technique of combining fruit, sugar, and lemon juice (for added pectin) and boiling to setting point is documented across a century of British preserving guides including the WI Home Preservation of Fruit and Vegetables and Pam Corbin's River Cottage Handbook No. 2: Preserves (2008). The wrinkle test for setting is the standard home jam check.

content.text: Strawberry jam has less natural pectin than most fruits, which is why recipes add lemon juice. The lemon also brightens the colour and sharpens the fruit flavour. The key to good strawberry jam is not to overcook it: a bright red, freshly-fruity result is the target. Jam that has been cooked too long turns dark, brown at the edges, and tastes of caramel rather than strawberries.

Sterilise four 340 g jars by washing in hot soapy water, rinsing, and placing upright in an oven at 120°C for 15 minutes. Place a small plate in the freezer for testing. Combine {{strawberries}} of hulled strawberries, {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar, and the juice of 2 lemons in a wide preserving pan or large saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar has fully dissolved; do not let it boil yet. Once dissolved, turn the heat to high and bring to a rolling boil. Boil hard for 8 to 12 minutes without stirring. Test for a set by dropping a teaspoon of jam onto the cold plate and pushing it with your finger, if it wrinkles, the jam is ready. Remove from the heat. Skim off any foam. Pour into the hot sterilised jars. Seal immediately. Leave to cool and store in a cool, dark place.

items.fix: Return to the pan, add the juice of one more lemon, and boil for 5 more minutes. Test again on the cold plate.
items.cause: Not reached setting point, or strawberries were very low in pectin.
items.symptom: Jam won't set, still runny after cooling

items.fix: Start testing at 8 minutes. Strawberry jam sets quickly, a minute or two past the wrinkle stage and the colour begins to darken.
items.symptom: Jam very dark and tastes of caramel

items.fix: Stir over low heat until every grain of sugar has dissolved and the liquid is completely clear before increasing the heat.

content.text: The wrinkle test is more reliable than a thermometer for home jam-making. Drop a teaspoon of hot jam onto the cold plate from the freezer and leave for 30 seconds. Push the blob with your finger: if the surface wrinkles and holds its shape, the jam has enough pectin and sugar concentration to set as it cools. If the surface stays flat and fluid, boil for two more minutes and test again. Always remove the pan from the heat during the test so the jam does not overcook while you are checking.
